questions.question: How do you find the largest element in an array? Write the approach and explain the time complexity.
questions.expected_answer: Initialize max with first element. Traverse array and update max whenever a larger value is found. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).
questions.explanation: Fundamental array iteration problem testing loop control and variable tracking.

questions.question: Explain how to reverse a string in-place without using built-in helper functions.
hints: Use two pointers at start and end
questions.expected_answer: Set left=0 and right=len-1. Swap chars at left and right, increment left and decrement right until left >= right.
questions.explanation: Demonstrates two-pointer technique for in-place string modification.

questions.question: What is the two-pointer technique? Explain with an example of finding a pair with a given sum in a sorted array.
hints: Start left at 0 and right at n-1
Adjust pointers based on sum comparison
O(n) time vs O(n^2) nested loops
questions.expected_answer: If arr[left] + arr[right] == target, return pair. If sum < target, left++. If sum > target, right--. Requires sorted array.
questions.explanation: Efficient O(n) approach for pair searching on sorted collections.

questions.question: How do you check if a string is a palindrome?
hints: Compare characters from outer ends inward
Stop when mismatch is found or center reached
questions.expected_answer: Compare char at index i with char at index n-1-i. If all match, it is a palindrome. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).
questions.explanation: Standard string checking problem frequently asked in campus interviews.

questions.question: What is the sliding window technique? Give a practical example.
hints: Reuse computed sum/window instead of recalculating
questions.expected_answer: Sliding window maintains a window of fixed or variable size, adding new elements and dropping old ones as it shifts right. Reduces nested loop O(n^2) to linear O(n).
questions.explanation: Essential technique for array and string subarray optimization problems.

questions.question: What is the difference between an Array and a Linked List? When would you prefer a Linked List?
hints: Arrays have contiguous memory, linked lists use pointers
questions.expected_answer: Arrays have contiguous memory and O(1) random access. Linked Lists have non-contiguous memory, O(n) access, but O(1) insertions/deletions once pointer is positioned.
questions.explanation: Core data structure trade-offs knowledge required for all placement interviews.

questions.question: How do you detect a cycle in a linked list using Floyd's Cycle Finding Algorithm?
hints: Use slow pointer (1 step) and fast pointer (2 steps)
If fast meets slow, a cycle exists
questions.expected_answer: Advance slow by 1 node and fast by 2 nodes. If fast reaches NULL, no cycle. If fast == slow, cycle detected. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).
questions.explanation: Classic interview problem for testing pointer manipulation.

questions.question: Explain how to reverse a singly linked list recursively and iteratively.
hints: Iterative: track prev, curr, next pointers
questions.expected_answer: Iterative: Maintain prev=None, curr=head. Loop: next=curr.next, curr.next=prev, prev=curr, curr=next. Return prev as new head. Recursive: Reverse sublist and fix tail pointer.

questions.question: How do you find the middle node of a linked list in a single traversal?
hints: When fast reaches end, slow is at the middle
questions.expected_answer: Move slow pointer by 1 step and fast pointer by 2 steps. When fast reaches end (NULL or fast.next is NULL), slow points to the middle node.
questions.explanation: Fast & slow pointer application without needing two passes.

questions.question: Explain the difference between Stack and Queue data structures with real-world examples.
questions.expected_answer: Stack: LIFO (e.g. undo history, stack of plates). Queue: FIFO (e.g. print queue, customer line). Stack uses push/pop; Queue uses enqueue/dequeue.

questions.question: How do you evaluate if a string containing parentheses '()[]{}' is valid and balanced using a Stack?
hints: On closing bracket, check if top of stack matches
questions.expected_answer: Push opening brackets onto stack. For closing bracket, pop top item and verify matching pair. If stack is empty at end, expression is valid. Time: O(n), Space: O(n).

questions.question: How can you implement a Queue using two Stacks?
hints: Use stack1 for enqueue, stack2 for dequeue
Transfer elements from stack1 to stack2 when stack2 is empty
questions.expected_answer: Enqueue: Push to stack1. Dequeue: If stack2 is empty, pop all items from stack1 and push to stack2, then pop stack2. Amortized time per operation: O(1).

questions.question: What is a Binary Search Tree (BST) and how is it different from a general Binary Tree?
questions.expected_answer: Binary Tree has no ordering rule for node values. BST enforces left subtree < node < right subtree, allowing O(log n) search in balanced trees.

questions.question: Explain Tree Traversal methods: Inorder, Preorder, and Postorder traversals.
questions.expected_answer: Inorder (L-Root-R) prints BST in sorted order. Preorder (Root-L-R) useful for copying tree structure. Postorder (L-R-Root) useful for bottom-up tree deletion.

questions.question: How do you calculate the height (max depth) of a Binary Tree recursively?
hints: Base case: height of NULL is 0
questions.expected_answer: If node is NULL, return 0. Else return 1 + max(height(node.left), height(node.right)). Time: O(n), Space: O(h) for call stack.

questions.question: What is a Hash Table and how does a Hash Function work?
hints: Maps key to index via hash code
questions.expected_answer: Hash Table uses a Hash Function to convert keys into array indices for O(1) average key-value lookup, insertion, and deletion.

questions.question: What is a Hash Collision and what are the two main collision handling techniques?
hints: Collision occurs when two keys map to same index
Open Addressing searches next free slot
questions.expected_answer: Collision occurs when two distinct keys yield the same index. Techniques: 1. Separate Chaining (linked list at index). 2. Open Addressing (linear probing, quadratic probing, double hashing).
questions.explanation: Essential internal working of hash maps.

questions.question: Explain how Binary Search works. What is its prerequisite?
questions.expected_answer: Prerequisite: Array must be sorted. Compare target to mid element. If target == mid, return index. If target < mid, search left half, else right half. Time: O(log n).

questions.question: Compare Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, and Insertion Sort in terms of time complexity.
hints: All have O(n^2) worst-case time complexity
Insertion sort is efficient for small/nearly sorted data
questions.expected_answer: All three have O(n^2) average and worst-case time complexity. Bubble Sort swaps adjacent elements; Selection Sort selects minimum element; Insertion Sort inserts into sorted subarray.

questions.question: Explain Merge Sort algorithm and why its time complexity is O(n log n).
hints: Splits array into halves, recursively sorts, then merges
questions.expected_answer: Merge Sort recursively divides array into two halves until size 1 (log n splits), then merges sorted halves in O(n) time. Total time: O(n log n), Space: O(n).
questions.explanation: Standard O(n log n) sorting algorithm.

questions.question: What is the difference between Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) in Graphs?
questions.expected_answer: BFS explores graph level by level using a Queue (ideal for shortest path in unweighted graph). DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking using a Stack/Recursion.

questions.question: What is Dynamic Programming? How is Memoization different from Tabulation?
questions.short_description: Dynamic Programming concept & Memoization vs Tabulation
hints: Used for overlapping subproblems & optimal substructure
Memoization is Top-Down, Tabulation is Bottom-Up
questions.expected_answer: DP solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems. Memoization is Top-Down (recursion + caching result). Tabulation is Bottom-Up (iterative table filling).

questions.question: What are ACID properties in DBMS? Explain each property with an example.
questions.expected_answer: Atomicity (all operations succeed or rollback), Consistency (valid state rules), Isolation (concurrent transactions execute independently), Durability (committed data survives system crashes).

questions.question: Explain Database Normalization and why 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF are needed.
questions.expected_answer: Normalization reduces data redundancy and eliminates update anomalies. 1NF (atomic attributes), 2NF (1NF + no non-key attribute depends on part of primary key), 3NF (2NF + no non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute).

questions.question: What is a Database Index? Compare Clustered and Non-Clustered Indexes.
hints: Index speeds up SELECT queries at cost of slower INSERT/UPDATE
Clustered index determines physical row order
questions.expected_answer: Index is a data structure (B-tree) for fast data retrieval. Clustered Index sorts physical data rows in table (only 1 per table). Non-Clustered Index stores logical order with pointers to actual rows (multiple allowed).
questions.explanation: Critical topic for database optimization questions.

questions.question: What is an ER Diagram? Define Entity, Attribute, and Primary vs Foreign Key.
questions.expected_answer: Entity represents real-world object. Attribute is a property. Primary Key uniquely identifies a record in a table. Foreign Key references Primary Key of another table to maintain referential integrity.

questions.question: Compare Relational Databases (SQL) and NoSQL Databases with examples.
hints: SQL has fixed schema and ACID support
NoSQL has dynamic schema (JSON/Key-Value) and horizontal scaling
questions.expected_answer: SQL (PostgreSQL, MySQL): Structured, tabular, schema-enforced, ACID compliant, vertical scaling. NoSQL (MongoDB, Redis): Unstructured/JSON, schema-less, BASE consistency, horizontal scaling.

questions.question: Explain the 4 main pillars of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP).
questions.expected_answer: 1. Encapsulation (bundling data & methods, hiding internal state). 2. Abstraction (showing only necessary features). 3. Inheritance (reusing parent class properties). 4. Polymorphism (one interface, multiple implementations).

questions.question: What is the difference between Abstraction and Encapsulation?
questions.expected_answer: Abstraction solves design-level complexity by hiding implementation details and showing essential features. Encapsulation solves application-level privacy by wrapping data and code into a single unit using access modifiers (private/protected).
questions.explanation: Differentiates two often confused core concepts.

questions.question: Compare Method Overloading and Method Overriding with examples.
hints: Overloading: Same method name, different parameters (compile-time)
Overriding: Child class redefines parent method (runtime)
questions.expected_answer: Overloading (Compile-Time Polymorphism): Methods in same class with same name but different parameter list. Overriding (Runtime Polymorphism): Child class provides specific implementation of method declared in parent class.

questions.question: What is an Interface and how does it differ from an Abstract Class?
hints: Abstract class can have state and non-abstract methods
questions.expected_answer: Abstract Class can have method implementations, state/member variables, and constructors. Interface contains abstract methods (contracts) without state (enabling multiple interface inheritance in languages like Java/C#).

questions.question: What are the SOLID principles in Object-Oriented Design? Explain the Single Responsibility Principle.
hints: SRP: A class should have only one reason to change
questions.expected_answer: SOLID stands for Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion. Single Responsibility Principle states a class should have one, and only one, reason to change.
questions.explanation: Clean code software architecture design principles.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Process and a Thread? Give real-world examples.
hints: Process has independent memory address space
Threads share memory within same process
questions.expected_answer: Process is an independent program in execution with private memory address space (e.g. Browser application). Thread is a lightweight execution path inside a process sharing its memory (e.g. background tab rendering).

questions.question: What is a Deadlock? Explain the 4 necessary conditions for a Deadlock to occur.
hints: Four conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
questions.expected_answer: Deadlock is a state where set of processes are blocked waiting for resources held by each other. Conditions: 1. Mutual Exclusion. 2. Hold and Wait. 3. No Preemption. 4. Circular Wait.
questions.explanation: Critical concurrency and OS resource allocation concept.

questions.question: What is Virtual Memory and Paging in Operating Systems?
hints: Allows running programs larger than physical RAM
Paging divides memory into fixed-size pages
questions.expected_answer: Virtual Memory provides illusion of large RAM by using secondary storage. Paging breaks virtual memory into fixed-size pages and physical RAM into page frames mapped by Page Table.

questions.question: Explain the 7 layers of the OSI Model and their functions.
hints: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
questions.expected_answer: 7 Layers: 1. Physical (bits). 2. Data Link (frames, MAC). 3. Network (packets, IP). 4. Transport (segments, TCP/UDP). 5. Session (dialogue control). 6. Presentation (encryption, formatting). 7. Application (HTTP, FTP, SMTP).

questions.question: Explain the TCP Three-Way Handshake process for establishing a connection.
questions.expected_answer: Step 1: Client sends SYN packet to Server. Step 2: Server responds with SYN-ACK packet. Step 3: Client responds with ACK packet. Connection established.
questions.explanation: Core reliable transport protocol connection mechanism.

questions.question: What are common HTTP request methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and HTTP status codes (200, 404, 500)?
hints: GET retrieves data, POST creates, PUT updates, DELETE removes
questions.expected_answer: Methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace/update), DELETE (remove). Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error.
